title: Scaling Your Database
intro: Your task manager is growing. Users are reporting that loading their task list takes 5+ seconds. The database is the bottleneck. How do you fix it without rewriting everything?

teach.title: When Your Database Gets Slow
teach.body: As your data grows, queries that were fast with 100 rows become slow with 100,000 rows. The usual culprits: missing indexes on columns you filter or sort by, queries that fetch more data than needed (SELECT *), and N+1 query problems where your code runs a separate query for each item in a list.

teach.title: Common Database Performance Fixes
teach.code:
SLOW: SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = 123
  → No index on user_id, scans every row

FAST: CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_user_id ON tasks(user_id);
  → Database jumps directly to matching rows

SLOW: Fetching 10,000 tasks then filtering in JavaScript
FAST: SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = 123 LIMIT 20 OFFSET 0;
  → Let the database do the filtering and pagination
teach.annotation: Indexes and pagination are the two most impactful database optimizations. They solve 80% of performance problems.

teach.body: Before adding infrastructure, optimize your queries. Add indexes on frequently filtered columns, use pagination, and select only the columns you need. These changes are free and often 10-100x faster.

start.narrative: Users report that loading their task list takes 8 seconds. You check the database and find that the tasks table has 500,000 rows. The query SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC has no index on user_id. What do you do first?

choices.text: Upgrade to a larger database server with more RAM and CPU
Add an index on the user_id column and add pagination to limit results to 20 per page
Switch to a NoSQL database like MongoDB because SQL can't handle this much data

throw-money.narrative: The bigger server helps temporarily — load times drop to 3 seconds. But the fundamental problem (no index, no pagination) remains. As data grows, you'll need an even bigger server and the costs keep climbing.
throw-money.feedback: Throwing hardware at a software problem is expensive and unsustainable. An index takes one line of SQL and solves the problem for free. Always optimize before scaling up.

add-index.narrative: You add an index on user_id and implement pagination (20 tasks per page). Load time drops from 8 seconds to 200ms. But you notice the task detail page is also slow — it loads the task plus all its comments in separate queries. What do you do?

choices.text: Use a JOIN query to fetch the task and its comments in a single database call
Cache the task details page since tasks don't change often

switch-db.narrative: Migrating to a different database is a massive undertaking — rewriting queries, changing the schema, migrating data. Three weeks later, you discover MongoDB has the same performance issues without indexes. The database type was never the problem.
switch-db.feedback: Switching databases rarely solves performance problems. The same indexing and query optimization principles apply everywhere. Optimize your current setup before considering a migration.

join-query.narrative: A single JOIN query replaces 20+ separate queries. The task detail page now loads in 150ms. Your database is humming along efficiently with proper indexes, pagination, and optimized queries.
join-query.feedback: This is textbook database optimization. Indexes, pagination, and JOIN queries solve the vast majority of database performance problems. No new infrastructure needed.

cache-approach.narrative: Caching helps, but you still have the N+1 query problem. Every cache miss is slow. You eventually add the JOIN query too, making the cache less necessary but still useful.
cache-approach.feedback: Caching is a valid strategy but it's treating the symptom, not the cause. Fix the underlying query first, then add caching as an enhancement.
